sections.title: Formation of Konjunktiv I
sections.explanation: Konjunktiv I is formed from the infinitive stem of the verb plus special endings. Take the infinitive, remove -en, and add: ich -e, du -est, er/sie/es -e, wir -en, ihr -et, sie/Sie -en. For example, 'gehen': ich gehe, du gehest, er gehe, wir gehen, ihr gehet, sie gehen. For 'haben': ich habe, du habest, er habe, wir haben, ihr habet, sie haben. The verb 'sein' is irregular: ich sei, du sei(e)st, er sei, wir seien, ihr seiet, sie seien. Notice that many Konjunktiv I forms are identical to the indicative (ich gehe, wir gehen). In those cases, Konjunktiv II is used as a replacement.
sections.tip: The most commonly used Konjunktiv I form is the 3rd person singular (er/sie/es), because it is always distinguishable from the indicative: er geht (indicative) vs. er gehe (Konjunktiv I).

exercises.prompt: What is the Konjunktiv I form of 'gehen' for er/sie/es?
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I of 'gehen' for er/sie/es: stem geh- + ending -e = er gehe. 'Er geht' is indicative, 'er ginge' is Konjunktiv II.

exercises.prompt: Konjunktiv I of 'haben' for er/sie/es: er/sie/es ___
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I: haben, stem hab- + -e = habe.

exercises.prompt: Match each verb to its Konjunktiv I form (er/sie/es):
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I is formed from the infinitive stem + -e for er/sie/es. 'Sein' is irregular: er sei (not er seie).

sections.title: Using Konjunktiv I in Reported Speech
sections.explanation: Konjunktiv I is primarily used in reported speech (indirekte Rede) to signal that you are reporting what someone else said, without confirming whether it is true. Direct speech: Er sagt: 'Ich bin krank.' Reported speech: Er sagt, er sei krank. The conjunction 'dass' can optionally be used: Er sagt, dass er krank sei. In formal writing and news reports, Konjunktiv I is standard. In everyday spoken German, people often use the indicative instead: Er sagt, er ist krank. Tenses shift: present becomes Konjunktiv I present (er gehe), past becomes Konjunktiv I past (er sei gegangen / er habe gesagt).
sections.tip: In news German, you will see Konjunktiv I constantly: 'Der Minister sagte, die Lage sei ernst.' This signals the journalist is reporting, not endorsing, the statement.

examples.en: He said he was sick. (reported speech)

examples.de: Sie behauptet, sie habe nichts gewusst.
examples.en: She claims she knew nothing. (reported speech)

examples.de: Der Arzt meint, das Kind brauche Ruhe.
examples.en: The doctor says the child needs rest. (reported speech)

examples.de: Die Zeitung berichtet, der Präsident komme morgen.
examples.en: The newspaper reports the president is coming tomorrow.

examples.de: Er sagte, er habe den Brief geschrieben.
examples.en: He said he had written the letter.

exercises.prompt: Er sagte, er ___ müde. (He said he was tired.) -- use Konjunktiv I of 'sein'
exercises.hint: Konjunktiv I of 'sein' for er = sei
exercises.explanation: In reported speech: Er sagte, er sei müde. 'Sei' is the Konjunktiv I of 'sein'.

exercises.prompt: Convert to reported speech: Maria sagt: 'Ich habe Hunger.' -- Maria sagt, sie ___ Hunger.
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I for reported speech: 'ich habe' (direct) becomes 'sie habe' (reported). 'Hat' is indicative, 'hätte' is Konjunktiv II.

exercises.prompt: Die Lehrerin sagte, die Schüler ___ mehr lernen. (The teacher said the students should study more.) -- Konjunktiv I of 'sollen'
exercises.hint: When Konjunktiv I is identical to indicative (sie sollen = sie sollen), use Konjunktiv II instead
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I of 'sollen' for sie (plural) would be 'sollen' -- identical to indicative. So we use Konjunktiv II: 'sollten'.

sections.title: When to Use Konjunktiv I vs. Konjunktiv II in Reported Speech
sections.explanation: The rule: Use Konjunktiv I in reported speech whenever it is clearly different from the indicative. When the Konjunktiv I form is identical to the indicative, replace it with Konjunktiv II. Examples: 'ich gehe' (indicative) = 'ich gehe' (Konj. I) -- identical! Use Konjunktiv II: 'ich ginge'. 'wir haben' (indicative) = 'wir haben' (Konj. I) -- identical! Use Konjunktiv II: 'wir hätten'. 'er geht' (indicative) is not the same as 'er gehe' (Konj. I) -- different! Use Konjunktiv I: 'er gehe'. The 3rd person singular (er/sie/es) almost always uses Konjunktiv I because it is always distinguishable. The 1st person singular and all plural forms often require Konjunktiv II as a substitute.
sections.tip: Practical shortcut: for er/sie/es, use Konjunktiv I. For ich, wir, sie (plural), you often need Konjunktiv II because Konjunktiv I looks the same as the indicative.

examples.de: Er sagte, er gehe nach Hause.
examples.en: He said he was going home. (Konj. I -- clearly different from 'geht')

examples.de: Er sagte, sie gingen nach Hause.
examples.en: He said they were going home. (Konj. II -- because Konj. I 'gehen' = indicative)

examples.de: Sie sagten, sie hätten kein Geld.
examples.en: They said they had no money. (Konj. II replaces Konj. I 'haben')

examples.de: Er meinte, er wisse die Antwort.
examples.en: He said he knew the answer. (Konj. I of 'wissen')

exercises.prompt: Reported speech: Sie sagten: 'Wir haben keine Zeit.' -- Sie sagten, sie ___ keine Zeit.
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I of 'haben' for sie (plural) is 'haben' -- identical to the indicative. So we use Konjunktiv II: 'hätten'.

exercises.prompt: Reported speech: Der Chef sagte: 'Die Firma wächst.' -- Der Chef sagte, die Firma ___.
exercises.explanation: For er/sie/es, Konjunktiv I is always different from indicative: die Firma wächst (ind.) becomes die Firma wachse (Konj. I).

exercises.prompt: Er sagte, er ___ das nicht. (He said he didn't know that.) -- Konjunktiv I of 'wissen' for er
exercises.explanation: Konjunktiv I: wissen becomes er wisse. This is clearly different from the indicative 'er weiss'.

exercises.prompt: Match each reported speech situation to the correct form:
exercises.explanation: When Konjunktiv I = indicative (wir gehen, sie haben), switch to Konjunktiv II (wir gingen, sie hätten).
